subject: English

lesson.story_mission: Join the Fluency Theater and help the cast read every word, phrase, and sentence clearly.
lesson.objective: I can read Grade 2 high-frequency words, phrases, and sentences accurately and smoothly.
lesson.concept_explanation: Fluent readers recognize common words automatically, read meaningful groups of words together, and use punctuation to make their voices match the text.
lesson.common_misconception: Readers may skip small words, pause after every word, or ignore punctuation at the end of a sentence.
lesson.real_world_connection: Fluency helps with reading directions, stories, poems, and classroom messages.
lesson.lesson_summary: Read each word accurately, group words in phrases, and stop or change voice for punctuation.
lesson.mini_lesson: First, check every letter in an unfamiliar word. Next, group words that make one idea. Then reread the whole sentence, using the punctuation to guide your pauses and expression.

worked_examples.text: Watch: The word because should be recognized quickly without sounding out every letter.
Demo: Read around the tree as one smooth phrase, not three choppy words.
Demo: A question mark in Can you help me? tells the reader to use a question voice.

hint_ladder: Look at every word, including small function words.
Read the words together in a smooth phrase.
Let punctuation tell your voice when to stop or ask.

misconception_bank.sight_word_automaticity_gap: Learner needs repeated exposure before the word is automatic.
misconception_bank.phrase_chunking_error: Learner reads word by word instead of in a meaningful phrase.
misconception_bank.skips_function_words: Learner skips small words that carry sentence meaning.
misconception_bank.punctuation_ignored: Learner reads through punctuation without changing pace or stopping.

game_theme.mission_intro: Practice word cards, phrase lights, and sentence signs before showtime.

level_banks.label: Level 1: Grade 2 Sight Word Set A
level_banks.focus: recognize Grade 2 high-frequency words quickly and accurately

questions.prompt: The class stayed inside ___ it was raining.
hints: Notice the words on both sides of the blank: stayed inside and it was raining.
The missing word should connect an event to its reason.
Read the completed rainy-day sentence; the two parts should have a clear reason link.
questions.explanation: Because connects what happened with the reason it happened.
questions.feedback: Because connects what happened with the reason it happened.

question_audio.text: The class stayed inside ___ it was raining.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: The class stayed inside ___ it was raining.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Wash your hands ___ you eat lunch.
hints: Picture the order of the two actions: washing hands and eating lunch.
The missing word should show that washing happens earlier.
Reread both actions in order and confirm that the time word fits.
questions.explanation: Before tells that washing happens earlier than eating.
questions.feedback: Before tells that washing happens earlier than eating.

question_audio.text: Wash your hands ___ you eat lunch.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Wash your hands ___ you eat lunch.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: We have ___ to this library many times.
hints: Read the helper word have before the blank.
Have pairs with a past-participle word to tell about an experience.
Read have plus your choice as one smooth verb phrase.
questions.explanation: Been works with have to tell about a place visited in the past.
questions.feedback: Been works with have to tell about a place visited in the past.

question_audio.text: We have ___ to this library many times.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: We have ___ to this library many times.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: What ___ this button do?
hints: Notice that the sentence asks about one button.
A question about one person or thing often uses a helper ending in s.
Say the whole question and listen for standard classroom English.
questions.explanation: Does helps ask what one thing can do.
questions.feedback: Does helps ask what one thing can do.

question_audio.text: What ___ this button do?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: What ___ this button do?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Mina ___ her missing mitten under the bench.
hints: Use the clue missing mitten under the bench.
Choose the past-tense action that means located.
Replace the blank and make sure the sentence tells where the mitten was located.
questions.explanation: Found means Mina located the mitten she had been seeking.
questions.feedback: Found means Mina located the mitten she had been seeking.

question_audio.text: Mina ___ her missing mitten under the bench.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Mina ___ her missing mitten under the bench.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: We walked ___ the puddle to keep our shoes dry.
hints: Picture the puddle and the goal of keeping shoes dry.
The direction word should show going beside the puddle, not through it.
Trace the imagined path, then reread to confirm the direction keeps shoes dry.
questions.explanation: Around shows that the walkers went beside the puddle instead of through it.
questions.feedback: Around shows that the walkers went beside the puddle instead of through it.

question_audio.text: We walked ___ the puddle to keep our shoes dry.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: We walked ___ the puddle to keep our shoes dry.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Turn ___ at the corner to reach the garden.
hints: Look for the direction named after the word Turn.
Choose the direction that is the opposite of left.
Face an imaginary corner and confirm the chosen direction matches the garden route.
questions.explanation: Right names the direction to turn at the corner.
questions.feedback: Right names the direction to turn at the corner.

question_audio.text: Turn ___ at the corner to reach the garden.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Turn ___ at the corner to reach the garden.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Please ___ the paintbrushes at the sink.
hints: Think about what removes paint from brushes at a sink.
Select the action verb that means clean with water.
Act out the direction mentally and confirm it makes sense at a sink.
questions.explanation: Wash is the action that cleans paint from the brushes.
questions.feedback: Wash is the action that cleans paint from the brushes.

question_audio.text: Please ___ the paintbrushes at the sink.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Please ___ the paintbrushes at the sink.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: The new leaves are bright ___.
hints: Look for the word that names the leaves’ color.
Only a color word can describe how the leaves look.
Read the noun and describing word together and check that the description is possible.
questions.explanation: Green is the color word that describes the new leaves.
questions.feedback: Green is the color word that describes the new leaves.

question_audio.text: The new leaves are bright ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: The new leaves are bright ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Bats ___ during much of the day.
hints: Use the clue rest during much of the day.
Choose the verb that names resting with eyes closed.
Reread the bat fact and confirm the action matches daytime rest.
questions.explanation: Sleep names what bats do while they rest during the day.
questions.feedback: Sleep names what bats do while they rest during the day.

question_audio.text: Bats ___ during much of the day.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Bats ___ during much of the day.
audio.label: Hear the text

level_banks.label: Level 2: Grade 2 Sight Word Set B
level_banks.focus: recognize a second set of Grade 2 high-frequency words

questions.prompt: Choose the correctly spelled word that completes the sentence: ___ puppy is playful.
hints: Look at the apostrophe-free choices and the word puppy after the blank.
The possessive word means belonging to them and follows the spelling t-h-e-i-r.
Compare every letter once more, then read the complete possessive phrase.
questions.explanation: Their is the correctly spelled word that shows the puppy belongs to them.
questions.feedback: Their is the correctly spelled word that shows the puppy belongs to them.

question_audio.text: Choose the correctly spelled word that completes the sentence: ___ puppy is playful.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the correctly spelled word that completes the sentence: ___ puppy is playful.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Choose the word that completes the polite question: ___ you share a crayon?
hints: Say the opening of the polite question slowly.
A polite request begins with the word spelled w-o-u-l-d.
Read the question aloud and check that it sounds polite and correctly spelled.
questions.explanation: Would begins a polite question; its letters appear in the correct order.
questions.feedback: Would begins a polite question; its letters appear in the correct order.

question_audio.text: Choose the word that completes the polite question: ___ you share a crayon?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the word that completes the polite question: ___ you share a crayon?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which word correctly completes the direction? ___ one fact about bees.
hints: Think about the action you do to put a fact on paper.
The writing action begins with the silent-letter team wr.
Spell the chosen action letter by letter, then reread the direction.
questions.explanation: Write means to put words on paper and begins with the silent-letter spelling wr.
questions.feedback: Write means to put words on paper and begins with the silent-letter spelling wr.

question_audio.text: Which word correctly completes the direction? ___ one fact about bees.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which word correctly completes the direction? ___ one fact about bees.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: The word card says many. Which sentence uses that exact word?
hints: Scan the four sentences for the exact five-letter word on the card.
Ignore sentences that change, remove, or add a letter to the card word.
Point to each letter in the card word and in the selected sentence to confirm a match.
questions.explanation: Many is read quickly as one word and tells that there were a large number of ants.
questions.feedback: Many is read quickly as one word and tells that there were a large number of ants.

question_audio.text: The word card says many. Which sentence uses that exact word?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: The word card says many. Which sentence uses that exact word?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Choose the exact word shown in this sentence: Those kites fly high.
hints: Compare each spelling with the word printed in the model sentence.
Track the letters t-h-o-s-e in that order.
Match the sentence word letter for letter before choosing.
questions.explanation: Those is the correctly spelled word in the sentence and points to more than one kite.
questions.feedback: Those is the correctly spelled word in the sentence and points to more than one kite.

question_audio.text: Choose the exact word shown in this sentence: Those kites fly high.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the exact word shown in this sentence: Those kites fly high.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which choice correctly spells the word that means every time?
hints: Look for one word that means every time.
The correct spelling has one l before ways and is written as one word.
Cover the choices, spell the word from memory, and compare.
questions.explanation: Always is one word with one l in the middle and means every time.
questions.feedback: Always is one word with one l in the middle and means every time.

question_audio.text: Which choice correctly spells the word that means every time?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which choice correctly spells the word that means every time?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: ___ teams carried water for the garden.
hints: The sentence is about two teams acting together.
The word for two together ends with th, not ht.
Reread the subject and verb; the choice should clearly refer to the two teams.
questions.explanation: Both means the two teams together and is spelled b-o-t-h.
questions.feedback: Both means the two teams together and is spelled b-o-t-h.

question_audio.text: ___ teams carried water for the garden.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: ___ teams carried water for the garden.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Wear a coat because the wind feels ___.
hints: Use the coat and chilly-wind clues.
The describing word needs the letters c-o-l-d in order.
Read the weather sentence and confirm both meaning and spelling.
questions.explanation: Cold describes the chilly wind and is the correctly spelled word.
questions.feedback: Cold describes the chilly wind and is the correctly spelled word.

question_audio.text: Wear a coat because the wind feels ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Wear a coat because the wind feels ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: The rabbit can run very ___.
hints: Ask how the rabbit runs.
The word meaning quickly uses the vowel a.
Put the choice after very and check that it tells how.
questions.explanation: Fast tells how quickly the rabbit runs.
questions.feedback: Fast tells how quickly the rabbit runs.

question_audio.text: The rabbit can run very ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: The rabbit can run very ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Use both hands to ___ the heavy door.
hints: Picture moving the heavy door toward you.
The action word ends with two l letters.
Pretend to use the door and confirm the chosen action moves it toward you.
questions.explanation: Pull means to move the door toward you and ends with two l letters.
questions.feedback: Pull means to move the door toward you and ends with two l letters.

question_audio.text: Use both hands to ___ the heavy door.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Use both hands to ___ the heavy door.
audio.label: Hear the text

level_banks.label: Level 3: Phrase Fluency
level_banks.focus: read short phrases smoothly without skipping small words

questions.prompt: Read the sentence in smooth groups. Which words belong together? We packed | ___ | for the hike.
hints: Ask what the hikers packed.
A smooth phrase keeps the complete list of supplies together.
Read We packed plus the phrase without unnecessary stops.
questions.explanation: A map and snacks forms one meaningful phrase naming what was packed.
questions.feedback: A map and snacks forms one meaningful phrase naming what was packed.

question_audio.text: Read the sentence in smooth groups. Which words belong together? We packed | ___ | for the hike.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Read the sentence in smooth groups. Which words belong together? We packed | ___ | for the hike.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which phrase smoothly completes the sentence? The kitten curled up ___.
hints: Ask where the kitten curled up.
A location phrase begins with a position word and stays together as one idea.
Read the complete sentence and listen for one smooth where group.
questions.explanation: Under the warm blanket is a meaningful group that tells where the kitten curled up.
questions.feedback: Under the warm blanket is a meaningful group that tells where the kitten curled up.

question_audio.text: Which phrase smoothly completes the sentence? The kitten curled up ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which phrase smoothly completes the sentence? The kitten curled up ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Choose the phrase that sounds natural: We cheered ___.
hints: Ask when the cheering happened.
A time phrase can answer when without scrambling its words.
Clap once for the main sentence and once for the time phrase; both should be meaningful.
questions.explanation: After the final goal belongs together because the words tell when the cheering happened.
questions.feedback: After the final goal belongs together because the words tell when the cheering happened.

question_audio.text: Choose the phrase that sounds natural: We cheered ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the phrase that sounds natural: We cheered ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Read in a smooth phrase: Dad set the bowl ___.
hints: Ask where Dad set the bowl.
Keep the position word, describing words, and place in natural English order.
Reread from Dad through the final word and check that the location sounds natural.
questions.explanation: On the kitchen table is a natural phrase that tells where Dad set the bowl.
questions.feedback: On the kitchen table is a natural phrase that tells where Dad set the bowl.

question_audio.text: Read in a smooth phrase: Dad set the bowl ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Read in a smooth phrase: Dad set the bowl ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which group completes the idea smoothly? The frogs croaked ___.
hints: Ask where the frogs croaked.
The whole location group should sound like one answer to where.
Read the phrase in one breath and verify that it answers where.
questions.explanation: Beside the quiet pond is one meaningful group that tells where the frogs croaked.
questions.feedback: Beside the quiet pond is one meaningful group that tells where the frogs croaked.

question_audio.text: Which group completes the idea smoothly? The frogs croaked ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which group completes the idea smoothly? The frogs croaked ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Select the natural phrase: Our bus arrived ___.
hints: Ask when the bus arrived.
Keep the time words together instead of pausing after every small word.
Reread the sentence with one gentle pause before the time group.
questions.explanation: Early in the morning flows as a phrase that tells when the bus arrived.
questions.feedback: Early in the morning flows as a phrase that tells when the bus arrived.

question_audio.text: Select the natural phrase: Our bus arrived ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Select the natural phrase: Our bus arrived ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which phrase should be read together? Kai carried ___.
hints: Ask what Kai carried and where he took it.
A clear phrase keeps the number, object, and destination in sensible order.
Read the full sentence and confirm that every word contributes to the idea.
questions.explanation: Three books to class groups the object and destination into a clear idea.
questions.feedback: Three books to class groups the object and destination into a clear idea.

question_audio.text: Which phrase should be read together? Kai carried ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which phrase should be read together? Kai carried ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Complete the sentence with the smooth phrase: Birds rested ___.
hints: Ask where the birds rested.
A location phrase starts with on and ends with the place.
Say the location phrase smoothly and check that its word order sounds natural.
questions.explanation: On a snowy branch is a meaningful phrase telling where the birds rested.
questions.feedback: On a snowy branch is a meaningful phrase telling where the birds rested.

question_audio.text: Complete the sentence with the smooth phrase: Birds rested ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Complete the sentence with the smooth phrase: Birds rested ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Choose the phrase that makes sense as one group: We listened ___.
hints: Ask what the group listened to.
The phrase should name the sound being heard in normal word order.
Reread the sentence and make sure the phrase clearly answers what was heard.
questions.explanation: To the gentle rain belongs together because it tells what we listened to.
questions.feedback: To the gentle rain belongs together because it tells what we listened to.

question_audio.text: Choose the phrase that makes sense as one group: We listened ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the phrase that makes sense as one group: We listened ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Read smoothly: Nia shared ___. Which phrase completes the sentence?
hints: Ask what Nia shared and with whom.
Keep the object and the with whom part in one meaningful group.
Read the completed sentence and confirm both the object and partner are clear.
questions.explanation: Her markers with Luis is a clear phrase naming what Nia shared and with whom.
questions.feedback: Her markers with Luis is a clear phrase naming what Nia shared and with whom.

question_audio.text: Read smoothly: Nia shared ___. Which phrase completes the sentence?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Read smoothly: Nia shared ___. Which phrase completes the sentence?
audio.label: Hear the text

level_banks.label: Level 4: Sentence Fluency
level_banks.focus: read simple sentences accurately and attend to punctuation

questions.prompt: Which end mark tells your voice to ask? Did you feed the fish___
hints: Read the sentence as a request for information.
A direct question ends with the mark that signals an asking voice.
Read the complete fish sentence with an asking voice and check the end mark.
questions.explanation: A question mark ends a direct question and signals an asking voice.
questions.feedback: A question mark ends a direct question and signals an asking voice.

question_audio.text: Which end mark tells your voice to ask? Did you feed the fish___
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which end mark tells your voice to ask? Did you feed the fish___
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which sentence should be read with strong excitement?
hints: Think about how your voice sounds after winning a relay.
Strong excitement is shown by an exclamation point, not a period.
Try each labeled voice; choose the one that matches both the event and punctuation.
questions.explanation: The exclamation point shows excitement, so the reader uses an energetic voice.
questions.feedback: The exclamation point shows excitement, so the reader uses an energetic voice.
choices: calm statement: We won the relay.
asking: Did we win the relay?

question_audio.text: Which sentence should be read with strong excitement?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which sentence should be read with strong excitement?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Choose the sentence with punctuation that signals a calm stop.
questions.correct_answer: calm stop: The moon looks bright.
hints: Decide whether the moon sentence tells, asks, or exclaims.
A calm telling sentence ends with a period.
Lower your voice at the end and confirm the sentence makes a calm statement.
questions.explanation: A period ends this telling sentence and signals a full, calm stop.
questions.feedback: A period ends this telling sentence and signals a full, calm stop.
choices: calm stop: The moon looks bright.
asking voice: The moon looks bright?
excited voice: The moon looks bright!
unfinished pause: The moon looks bright,

question_audio.text: Choose the sentence with punctuation that signals a calm stop.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the sentence with punctuation that signals a calm stop.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which punctuation helps a reader pause after First? ___ mix the flour and water.
hints: Notice the opening transition word First.
A comma after an opening transition signals a short pause.
Pause briefly after the transition, then continue the direction.
questions.explanation: A comma after First marks a short pause before the rest of the direction.
questions.feedback: A comma after First marks a short pause before the rest of the direction.

question_audio.text: Which punctuation helps a reader pause after First? ___ mix the flour and water.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which punctuation helps a reader pause after First? ___ mix the flour and water.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which sentence should end with an asking voice?
questions.correct_answer: asking voice: Where is my blue cap?
hints: The word Where signals that the speaker wants information.
A question mark matches the asking purpose of a Where sentence.
Read the cap sentence aloud and confirm that your voice asks at the end.
questions.explanation: Where begins a question, and the question mark tells the voice to rise or sound curious.
questions.feedback: Where begins a question, and the question mark tells the voice to rise or sound curious.
choices: telling voice: Where is my blue cap.
excited voice: Where is my blue cap!
asking voice: Where is my blue cap?
pause in the middle: Where is, my blue cap?

question_audio.text: Which sentence should end with an asking voice?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which sentence should end with an asking voice?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Select the sentence whose end mark matches a surprised voice.
hints: Picture the sudden appearance of a rainbow.
An exclamation point can show strong surprise.
Use a surprised voice and verify that the end mark supports it.
questions.explanation: The exclamation point matches the speaker’s strong surprise about the rainbow.
questions.feedback: The exclamation point matches the speaker’s strong surprise about the rainbow.

question_audio.text: Select the sentence whose end mark matches a surprised voice.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Select the sentence whose end mark matches a surprised voice.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Where should the reader make a brief pause? After lunch we planted seeds.
hints: Read the opening time phrase After lunch.
An opening time phrase is grouped together before a brief pause.
Pause after the full time phrase, not between its two words.
questions.explanation: The introductory words After lunch form one group, so a brief pause comes after lunch.
questions.feedback: The introductory words After lunch form one group, so a brief pause comes after lunch.

question_audio.text: Where should the reader make a brief pause? After lunch we planted seeds.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Where should the reader make a brief pause? After lunch we planted seeds.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which version helps a reader hear two items clearly?
hints: Find the clearest way to join the two craft supplies.
The joining word and makes the two-item list smooth and complete.
Read the supply sentence and confirm that both needed items are easy to hear.
questions.explanation: And joins the final two items so the reader can group the complete list clearly.
questions.feedback: And joins the final two items so the reader can group the complete list clearly.

question_audio.text: Which version helps a reader hear two items clearly?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which version helps a reader hear two items clearly?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which sentence uses punctuation to show that Ana is speaking?
questions.correct_answer: exact words: Ana said “I am ready.”
hints: Find the words Ana actually says.
Quotation marks belong immediately before and after a speaker’s exact words.
Read only the words inside the marks and verify that they are Ana’s speech.
questions.explanation: Quotation marks surround Ana’s exact words, and the period closes what she says.
questions.feedback: Quotation marks surround Ana’s exact words, and the period closes what she says.
choices: no quotation marks: Ana said I am ready.
comma only: Ana said, I am ready.
exact words: Ana said “I am ready.”
question ending: Ana said “I am ready”?

question_audio.text: Which sentence uses punctuation to show that Ana is speaking?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which sentence uses punctuation to show that Ana is speaking?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: How should you read Stop! in the sentence “Stop! The ball is rolling away”?
hints: Think about why someone shouts Stop when a ball rolls away.
An exclamation point makes the one-word command sound urgent.
Say Stop as a safety warning, pause, and then read the next sentence.
questions.explanation: The exclamation point makes Stop a strong warning, followed by a clear pause.
questions.feedback: The exclamation point makes Stop a strong warning, followed by a clear pause.

question_audio.text: How should you read Stop! in the sentence “Stop! The ball is rolling away”?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: How should you read Stop! in the sentence “Stop! The ball is rolling away”?
audio.label: Hear the text

level_banks.label: Mixed
level_banks.focus: Interleave all Grade 2 Sight Words and Fluency targets.

questions.prompt: The baker will ___ the rolls on a tray.
hints: Use the action clue: the baker puts rolls onto a tray.
Choose the verb that means put, not a sound-alike noun.
Read the bakery sentence and check that the action makes sense with on a tray.
questions.explanation: Place is the high-frequency word meaning to put something in a position.
questions.feedback: Place is the high-frequency word meaning to put something in a position.

question_audio.text: The baker will ___ the rolls on a tray.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: The baker will ___ the rolls on a tray.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Choose the correctly spelled word: We ___ our neighbors at the picnic.
hints: The picnic happened in the past, so look for the past-tense word.
The silent-k spelling is the past tense of know.
Use the word in the picnic sentence and verify that it tells about past knowledge.
questions.explanation: Knew is the past-tense word meaning already had knowledge of someone.
questions.feedback: Knew is the past-tense word meaning already had knowledge of someone.

question_audio.text: Choose the correctly spelled word: We ___ our neighbors at the picnic.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the correctly spelled word: We ___ our neighbors at the picnic.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which smooth phrase completes the sentence? The turtle moved ___.
hints: Ask where the turtle moved.
Keep the direction phrase in normal order as one where group.
Read the whole turtle sentence and listen for a smooth location group.
questions.explanation: Across the sandy path is a meaningful phrase telling where the turtle moved.
questions.feedback: Across the sandy path is a meaningful phrase telling where the turtle moved.

question_audio.text: Which smooth phrase completes the sentence? The turtle moved ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which smooth phrase completes the sentence? The turtle moved ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which end mark tells the reader this is a question? May I open the window___
hints: Decide whether May I asks, tells, or exclaims.
A polite request still needs a question mark.
Read the window request aloud and confirm that the end mark matches your asking voice.
questions.explanation: A question mark fits the request and signals an asking voice.
questions.feedback: A question mark fits the request and signals an asking voice.

question_audio.text: Which end mark tells the reader this is a question? May I open the window___
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which end mark tells the reader this is a question? May I open the window___
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Choose the word that completes the sentence: The children made the cards by ___.
hints: The children made the cards without someone doing it for them.
The plural reflexive word ends in selves and is written as one word.
Reread the sentence and verify that the word refers back to more than one child.
questions.explanation: Themselves means the children did the work without someone doing it for them.
questions.feedback: Themselves means the children did the work without someone doing it for them.

question_audio.text: Choose the word that completes the sentence: The children made the cards by ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Choose the word that completes the sentence: The children made the cards by ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Select the sentence with the correctly spelled word.
hints: Look closely at the middle letters in the action word.
The standard spelling doubles the r before the y.
Spell the action aloud, then check it in the complete basket sentence.
questions.explanation: Carry is spelled with two r letters and means to hold something while moving it.
questions.feedback: Carry is spelled with two r letters and means to hold something while moving it.

question_audio.text: Select the sentence with the correctly spelled word.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Select the sentence with the correctly spelled word.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which words form a natural phrase? The family ate ___.
hints: Ask where the family ate.
A location group beginning with beneath should stay in natural order.
Read the complete picnic sentence and confirm that the phrase answers where.
questions.explanation: Beneath the shady oak belongs together as a phrase telling where the family ate.
questions.feedback: Beneath the shady oak belongs together as a phrase telling where the family ate.

question_audio.text: Which words form a natural phrase? The family ate ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which words form a natural phrase? The family ate ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which sentence should be read with joyful excitement?
hints: Picture how a reader might react when ducklings hatch.
An exclamation point matches a joyful reaction.
Try the joyful voice and make sure its end mark fits the happy event.
questions.explanation: The exclamation point signals the joyful, excited voice that matches the event.
questions.feedback: The exclamation point signals the joyful, excited voice that matches the event.

question_audio.text: Which sentence should be read with joyful excitement?
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which sentence should be read with joyful excitement?
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Read smoothly. Which phrase completes the direction? Put your folder ___.
hints: Ask exactly where the folder should go.
Keep next to and the named place together as one location phrase.
Follow the direction in your mind and confirm that the folder’s location is exact.
questions.explanation: Next to the red bin is a smooth phrase telling exactly where to put the folder.
questions.feedback: Next to the red bin is a smooth phrase telling exactly where to put the folder.

question_audio.text: Read smoothly. Which phrase completes the direction? Put your folder ___.
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Read smoothly. Which phrase completes the direction? Put your folder ___.
audio.label: Hear the text

questions.prompt: Which punctuation marks Jamal’s exact words? Jamal said ___The show begins now.___
hints: Find the two marks that surround Jamal’s spoken words.
A matched pair of quotation marks identifies exact speech.
Read the words between the marks and verify that only Jamal’s exact speech is enclosed.
questions.explanation: Quotation marks go before and after Jamal’s exact spoken words.
questions.feedback: Quotation marks go before and after Jamal’s exact spoken words.

question_audio.text: Which punctuation marks Jamal’s exact words? Jamal said ___The show begins now.___
question_audio.label: Read Question

audio.text: Which punctuation marks Jamal’s exact words? Jamal said ___The show begins now.___
audio.label: Hear the text

lesson.text: This lesson teaches Grade 2 Sight Words and Fluency. Look for the reading or writing pattern on the page. Listen for word parts, important details, or sentence choices that help you decide. Remember to reread before you answer. Press Next to continue.
